title: Precalculus
blurb: Unit-by-unit practice for the Precalculus course — functions and transformations through conics.

units.title: Functions & Their Graphs
units.blurb: Domain, range, and graph features; composition; inverse functions; even/odd symmetry.

units.title: Transformations of Graphs
units.blurb: Shifts, stretches, reflections, the combined form a·f(x−h)+k, and piecewise graphs.

units.title: Polynomial Functions
units.blurb: End behavior, zeros and the Factor Theorem, multiplicity, division and the Remainder Theorem.

units.title: Rational Functions
units.blurb: Vertical asymptotes, holes, horizontal and slant asymptotes, and complete graphs.

units.title: Exponentials & Logarithms
units.blurb: Growth and decay, compound interest and e, logs as inverses, and exponential/log equations.

units.title: The Unit Circle
units.blurb: Radians, sine and cosine as coordinates, special angles, and reference angles in all quadrants.

units.title: Trig Graphs & Equations
units.blurb: The sine wave, amplitude/period/shifts, fundamental identities, and solving trig equations.

units.title: Conic Sections
units.blurb: Circles, ellipses, parabolas with foci, hyperbolas, and classifying second-degree equations.

forms.title: Evaluating compositions
forms.skill: f(g(k)): work inside out — evaluate g first, then feed it to f.

variants.explanation: Work inside out: $g(2) = 5$, then $f(5) = 13$. Composing in the wrong order, $g(f(2))$, gives $50$.
Work inside out: $f(1) = 4$, then $g(4) = 18$. Composing in the wrong order, $f(g(1))$, gives $10$.
Work inside out: $g(2) = 6$, then $f(6) = 25$. Composing in the wrong order, $g(f(2))$, gives $83$.
Work inside out: $f(-2) = -4$, then $g(-4) = 20$. Composing in the wrong order, $f(g(-2))$, gives $26$.
Work inside out: $g(3) = 14$, then $f(14) = 29$. Composing in the wrong order, $g(f(3))$, gives $54$.
Work inside out: $f(1) = 7$, then $g(7) = 50$. Composing in the wrong order, $f(g(1))$, gives $12$.
Work inside out: $g(2) = 7$, then $f(7) = 21$. Composing in the wrong order, $g(f(2))$, gives $124$.
Work inside out: $f(-2) = -5$, then $g(-5) = 26$. Composing in the wrong order, $f(g(-2))$, gives $23$.
Work inside out: $g(1) = 5$, then $f(5) = 26$. Composing in the wrong order, $g(f(1))$, gives $40$.
Work inside out: $f(3) = 8$, then $g(8) = 70$. Composing in the wrong order, $f(g(3))$, gives $32$.
Work inside out: $g(2) = 6$, then $f(6) = 23$. Composing in the wrong order, $g(f(2))$, gives $123$.
Work inside out: $f(-1) = -2$, then $g(-2) = 7$. Composing in the wrong order, $f(g(-1))$, gives $18$.

forms.title: Average rate of change
forms.skill: Slope of the secant: (f(q) − f(p))/(q − p) — for x² + c it collapses to p + q.

variants.statement: For $f(x) = x^2 + 3$, find the average rate of change of $f$ from $x = 1$ to $x = 4$.
variants.explanation: The average rate is $\frac{f(4) - f(1)}{4 - 1} = \frac{15}{3} = 5$. Stopping at $f(4) - f(1) = 15$ — forgetting to divide by the run — is the classic slip.

variants.statement: For $f(x) = x^2 - 2$, find the average rate of change of $f$ from $x = 2$ to $x = 5$.
variants.explanation: The average rate is $\frac{f(5) - f(2)}{5 - 2} = \frac{21}{3} = 7$. Stopping at $f(5) - f(2) = 21$ — forgetting to divide by the run — is the classic slip.

variants.explanation: The average rate is $\frac{f(5) - f(3)}{5 - 3} = \frac{16}{2} = 8$. Stopping at $f(5) - f(3) = 16$ — forgetting to divide by the run — is the classic slip.

forms.title: Domains with roots and fractions
forms.skill: Intersect the conditions: the radicand stays ≥ 0 AND the denominator stays ≠ 0.

variants.statement: What is the domain of $f(x) = \dfrac{\sqrt{x - 1}}{x - 4}$?
variants.explanation: The square root needs $x - 1 \ge 0$ and the denominator forbids $x = 4$, so $x \ge 1$ with $x \neq 4$. Dropping the denominator condition — the classic slip — keeps the forbidden point $x = 4$.

variants.statement: What is the domain of $f(x) = \dfrac{\sqrt{x - 2}}{x - 5}$?
variants.explanation: The square root needs $x - 2 \ge 0$ and the denominator forbids $x = 5$, so $x \ge 2$ with $x \neq 5$. Dropping the denominator condition — the classic slip — keeps the forbidden point $x = 5$.

forms.title: Naming the transformation
forms.skill: Inside the parentheses moves opposite its sign; outside moves as written; minus reflects.

variants.statement: How is the graph of $y = f(x - 3) + 2$ obtained from the graph of $y = f(x)$?
variants.explanation: Inside the parentheses, $x - 3$ moves the graph right $3$ (opposite the visible sign); the $+ 2$ outside moves it up $2$. Reading the inner sign literally — left $3$ — is the classic trap.

variants.statement: How is the graph of $y = f(x + 4) + 5$ obtained from the graph of $y = f(x)$?
variants.explanation: Inside the parentheses, $x + 4$ moves the graph left $4$ (opposite the visible sign); the $+ 5$ outside moves it up $5$. Reading the inner sign literally — right $4$ — is the classic trap.

variants.statement: How is the graph of $y = f(x - 2) - 6$ obtained from the graph of $y = f(x)$?
variants.explanation: Inside the parentheses, $x - 2$ moves the graph right $2$ (opposite the visible sign); the $- 6$ outside moves it down $6$. Reading the inner sign literally — left $2$ — is the classic trap.

variants.statement: How is the graph of $y = f(x + 1) - 3$ obtained from the graph of $y = f(x)$?
variants.explanation: Inside the parentheses, $x + 1$ moves the graph left $1$ (opposite the visible sign); the $- 3$ outside moves it down $3$. Reading the inner sign literally — right $1$ — is the classic trap.

variants.statement: How is the graph of $y = f(x - 5) + 4$ obtained from the graph of $y = f(x)$?
variants.explanation: Inside the parentheses, $x - 5$ moves the graph right $5$ (opposite the visible sign); the $+ 4$ outside moves it up $4$. Reading the inner sign literally — left $5$ — is the classic trap.

variants.statement: How is the graph of $y = f(x + 2) + 7$ obtained from the graph of $y = f(x)$?
variants.explanation: Inside the parentheses, $x + 2$ moves the graph left $2$ (opposite the visible sign); the $+ 7$ outside moves it up $7$. Reading the inner sign literally — right $2$ — is the classic trap.

variants.statement: How is the graph of $y = f(x - 4) - 1$ obtained from the graph of $y = f(x)$?
variants.explanation: Inside the parentheses, $x - 4$ moves the graph right $4$ (opposite the visible sign); the $- 1$ outside moves it down $1$. Reading the inner sign literally — left $4$ — is the classic trap.

variants.statement: How is the graph of $y = f(x + 6) - 2$ obtained from the graph of $y = f(x)$?
variants.explanation: Inside the parentheses, $x + 6$ moves the graph left $6$ (opposite the visible sign); the $- 2$ outside moves it down $2$. Reading the inner sign literally — right $6$ — is the classic trap.

variants.statement: How is the graph of $y = f(x - 7) + 1$ obtained from the graph of $y = f(x)$?
variants.explanation: Inside the parentheses, $x - 7$ moves the graph right $7$ (opposite the visible sign); the $+ 1$ outside moves it up $1$. Reading the inner sign literally — left $7$ — is the classic trap.

variants.statement: How is the graph of $y = f(x + 5) - 4$ obtained from the graph of $y = f(x)$?
variants.explanation: Inside the parentheses, $x + 5$ moves the graph left $5$ (opposite the visible sign); the $- 4$ outside moves it down $4$. Reading the inner sign literally — right $5$ — is the classic trap.

variants.statement: How is the graph of $y = -f(x)$ obtained from the graph of $y = f(x)$?
variants.explanation: The minus sign acts on the output, flipping every $y$-value: a reflection across the x-axis. Flipping across the y-axis instead would be $y = f(-x)$ — the sign acting on the input.

variants.statement: How is the graph of $y = f(-x)$ obtained from the graph of $y = f(x)$?
variants.explanation: The minus sign acts on the input, flipping every $x$-value: a reflection across the y-axis. Flipping across the x-axis instead would be $y = -f(x)$ — the sign acting on the output.

forms.title: Tracking a point through a transform
forms.skill: y = a·f(x − h): the x-coordinate shifts by h, the y-coordinate stretches by a.

variants.statement: The point $(1; 6)$ lies on the graph of $y = f(x)$. Which point must lie on the graph of $y = 2f(x - 3)$?
variants.explanation: The inside $x - 3$ shifts $x$ by $+3$ and the outside factor $2$ stretches $y$, so $(1; 6) \to (4; 12)$. Shifting the wrong way gives $(-2; 12)$ — the classic inner-sign slip.

variants.statement: The point $(2; 4)$ lies on the graph of $y = f(x)$. Which point must lie on the graph of $y = 2f(x + 3)$?
variants.explanation: The inside $x + 3$ shifts $x$ by $-3$ and the outside factor $2$ stretches $y$, so $(2; 4) \to (-1; 8)$. Shifting the wrong way gives $(5; 8)$ — the classic inner-sign slip.

variants.statement: The point $(3; -6)$ lies on the graph of $y = f(x)$. Which point must lie on the graph of $y = 3f(x - 2)$?
variants.explanation: The inside $x - 2$ shifts $x$ by $+2$ and the outside factor $3$ stretches $y$, so $(3; -6) \to (5; -18)$. Shifting the wrong way gives $(1; -18)$ — the classic inner-sign slip.

variants.statement: The point $(-2; 8)$ lies on the graph of $y = f(x)$. Which point must lie on the graph of $y = 4f(x - 1)$?
variants.explanation: The inside $x - 1$ shifts $x$ by $+1$ and the outside factor $4$ stretches $y$, so $(-2; 8) \to (-1; 32)$. Shifting the wrong way gives $(-3; 32)$ — the classic inner-sign slip.

variants.statement: The point $(0; 6)$ lies on the graph of $y = f(x)$. Which point must lie on the graph of $y = 3f(x - 4)$?
variants.explanation: The inside $x - 4$ shifts $x$ by $+4$ and the outside factor $3$ stretches $y$, so $(0; 6) \to (4; 18)$. Shifting the wrong way gives $(-4; 18)$ — the classic inner-sign slip.

variants.statement: The point $(4; -4)$ lies on the graph of $y = f(x)$. Which point must lie on the graph of $y = 2f(x - 5)$?
variants.explanation: The inside $x - 5$ shifts $x$ by $+5$ and the outside factor $2$ stretches $y$, so $(4; -4) \to (9; -8)$. Shifting the wrong way gives $(-1; -8)$ — the classic inner-sign slip.

variants.explanation: The inside $x + 2$ shifts $x$ by $-2$ and the outside factor $3$ stretches $y$, so $(-1; 9) \to (-3; 27)$. Shifting the wrong way gives $(1; 27)$ — the classic inner-sign slip.

variants.explanation: The inside $x - 3$ shifts $x$ by $+3$ and the outside factor $4$ stretches $y$, so $(5; -8) \to (8; -32)$. Shifting the wrong way gives $(2; -32)$ — the classic inner-sign slip.

variants.statement: The point $(1; -10)$ lies on the graph of $y = f(x)$. Which point must lie on the graph of $y = 5f(x + 1)$?
variants.explanation: The inside $x + 1$ shifts $x$ by $-1$ and the outside factor $5$ stretches $y$, so $(1; -10) \to (0; -50)$. Shifting the wrong way gives $(2; -50)$ — the classic inner-sign slip.

forms.title: Building a quadratic from its zeros
forms.skill: Monic with zeros r, s: x² − (r + s)x + rs — sum with a minus, product as-is.

variants.statement: A monic quadratic has zeros $3$ and $-5$. What is its constant term?
variants.explanation: $(x - (3))(x - (-5))$ multiplies out to $x^2 + 2x - 15$, so the constant term is $rs = -15$. Answering $-(r + s) = 2$ mixes it up with the $x$-coefficient.

variants.statement: A monic quadratic has zeros $2$ and $7$. What is the coefficient of $x$?
variants.explanation: $(x - (2))(x - (7))$ multiplies out to $x^2 - 9x + 14$, so the $x$-coefficient is $-(r + s) = -9$. Forgetting the minus sign gives $9$.

variants.statement: A monic quadratic has zeros $-4$ and $6$. What is its constant term?
variants.explanation: $(x - (-4))(x - (6))$ multiplies out to $x^2 - 2x - 24$, so the constant term is $rs = -24$. Answering $-(r + s) = -2$ mixes it up with the $x$-coefficient.

variants.explanation: $(x - (5))(x - (2))$ multiplies out to $x^2 - 7x + 10$, so the constant term is $rs = 10$. Answering $-(r + s) = -7$ mixes it up with the $x$-coefficient.

variants.statement: A monic quadratic has zeros $-3$ and $-7$. What is the coefficient of $x$?
variants.explanation: $(x - (-3))(x - (-7))$ multiplies out to $x^2 + 10x + 21$, so the $x$-coefficient is $-(r + s) = 10$. Forgetting the minus sign gives $-10$.

variants.statement: A monic quadratic has zeros $4$ and $9$. What is its constant term?
variants.explanation: $(x - (4))(x - (9))$ multiplies out to $x^2 - 13x + 36$, so the constant term is $rs = 36$. Answering $-(r + s) = -13$ mixes it up with the $x$-coefficient.

variants.statement: A monic quadratic has zeros $-2$ and $5$. What is the coefficient of $x$?
variants.explanation: $(x - (-2))(x - (5))$ multiplies out to $x^2 - 3x - 10$, so the $x$-coefficient is $-(r + s) = -3$. Forgetting the minus sign gives $3$.

variants.explanation: $(x - (6))(x - (-1))$ multiplies out to $x^2 - 5x - 6$, so the constant term is $rs = -6$. Answering $-(r + s) = -5$ mixes it up with the $x$-coefficient.

variants.explanation: $(x - (-5))(x - (8))$ multiplies out to $x^2 - 3x - 40$, so the $x$-coefficient is $-(r + s) = -3$. Forgetting the minus sign gives $3$.

variants.explanation: $(x - (7))(x - (3))$ multiplies out to $x^2 - 10x + 21$, so the constant term is $rs = 21$. Answering $-(r + s) = -10$ mixes it up with the $x$-coefficient.

forms.title: Division and the remainder
forms.skill: Dividing by (x − k) leaves remainder p(k) — substitute instead of long-dividing.

variants.statement: Divide $x^3 + 2x^2 + 12x + 15$ by $(x - 2)$. What is the remainder?
variants.explanation: By the Remainder Theorem the remainder is $p(2) = 55$ — no long division needed. Evaluating at $x = -2$ (misreading the divisor's sign) gives $-9$.

variants.statement: Divide $x^3 - 3x^2 + 14x - 10$ by $(x - 1)$. What is the remainder?
variants.explanation: By the Remainder Theorem the remainder is $p(1) = 2$ — no long division needed. Evaluating at $x = -1$ (misreading the divisor's sign) gives $-28$.

variants.statement: Divide $x^3 + 5x^2 - 11x + 20$ by $(x + 2)$. What is the remainder?
variants.explanation: By the Remainder Theorem the remainder is $p(-2) = 54$ — no long division needed. Evaluating at $x = 2$ (misreading the divisor's sign) gives $26$.

variants.statement: Divide $x^3 + x^2 + 10x - 12$ by $(x - 3)$. What is the remainder?
variants.explanation: By the Remainder Theorem the remainder is $p(3) = 54$ — no long division needed. Evaluating at $x = -3$ (misreading the divisor's sign) gives $-60$.

variants.statement: Divide $x^3 - 2x^2 + 13x + 18$ by $(x + 1)$. What is the remainder?
variants.explanation: By the Remainder Theorem the remainder is $p(-1) = 2$ — no long division needed. Evaluating at $x = 1$ (misreading the divisor's sign) gives $30$.

variants.statement: Divide $x^3 + 4x^2 - 15x + 11$ by $(x - 2)$. What is the remainder?
variants.explanation: By the Remainder Theorem the remainder is $p(2) = 5$ — no long division needed. Evaluating at $x = -2$ (misreading the divisor's sign) gives $49$.

variants.statement: Divide $x^3 + 6x^2 + 12x - 14$ by $(x + 3)$. What is the remainder?
variants.explanation: By the Remainder Theorem the remainder is $p(-3) = -23$ — no long division needed. Evaluating at $x = 3$ (misreading the divisor's sign) gives $103$.

variants.statement: Divide $x^3 - 4x^2 + 16x + 10$ by $(x - 1)$. What is the remainder?
variants.explanation: By the Remainder Theorem the remainder is $p(1) = 23$ — no long division needed. Evaluating at $x = -1$ (misreading the divisor's sign) gives $-11$.

variants.statement: Divide $x^3 + 3x^2 - 13x + 25$ by $(x + 2)$. What is the remainder?
variants.explanation: By the Remainder Theorem the remainder is $p(-2) = 55$ — no long division needed. Evaluating at $x = 2$ (misreading the divisor's sign) gives $19$.

variants.statement: Divide $x^3 + 2x^2 + 11x - 16$ by $(x - 4)$. What is the remainder?
variants.explanation: By the Remainder Theorem the remainder is $p(4) = 124$ — no long division needed. Evaluating at $x = -4$ (misreading the divisor's sign) gives $-92$.

variants.statement: Divide $x^3 - 5x^2 + 18x + 12$ by $(x + 1)$. What is the remainder?
variants.explanation: By the Remainder Theorem the remainder is $p(-1) = -12$ — no long division needed. Evaluating at $x = 1$ (misreading the divisor's sign) gives $26$.

variants.statement: Divide $x^3 + 7x^2 - 12x + 30$ by $(x - 2)$. What is the remainder?
variants.explanation: By the Remainder Theorem the remainder is $p(2) = 42$ — no long division needed. Evaluating at $x = -2$ (misreading the divisor's sign) gives $74$.

forms.title: Intercepts of rational functions
forms.skill: x-intercept: numerator = 0. y-intercept: plug in x = 0 and watch the signs cancel.

variants.statement: For $f(x) = \dfrac{x - 3}{x - 7}$, what is the x-intercept?
variants.explanation: The graph crosses the x-axis where the numerator is zero: $x = 3$. The denominator's zero, $x = 7$, is the vertical asymptote — the classic mix-up.

variants.statement: For $f(x) = \dfrac{x - 2}{x - 5}$, what is the y-intercept?
variants.explanation: Substitute $x = 0$: $f(0) = \frac{0 - 2}{0 - 5} = \frac{2}{5}$ — the two minus signs cancel. Keeping one of them gives $-\frac{2}{5}$.

forms.title: Slant asymptotes
forms.skill: Degree over degree-minus-one: divide, keep the linear quotient, drop the remainder.

variants.statement: Find the slant asymptote of $f(x) = \dfrac{x^2 + 3x + 1}{x - 2}$.
variants.explanation: Long division gives $f(x) = x + 5 + \frac{11}{x - 2}$, and the fraction dies as $x \to \pm\infty$, so the slant asymptote is $y = x + 5$. Reading the intercept straight off the numerator as $3$ skips the division. ($x = 2$ is the vertical asymptote.)

variants.statement: Find the slant asymptote of $f(x) = \dfrac{x^2 - 2x + 4}{x - 3}$.

forms.title: Evaluating exponential models
forms.skill: A · b^x: exponentiate the base first, then scale by A; negative x means reciprocal.

variants.statement: If $f(x) = 3 \cdot 2^x$, find $f(4)$.
variants.explanation: $f(4) = 3 \cdot 2^{4} = 3 \cdot 16 = 48$. Multiplying instead of exponentiating — $3 \cdot 2 \cdot 4$ — gives $24$.

variants.statement: If $f(x) = 5 \cdot 3^x$, find $f(2)$.
variants.explanation: $f(2) = 5 \cdot 3^{2} = 5 \cdot 9 = 45$. Multiplying instead of exponentiating — $5 \cdot 3 \cdot 2$ — gives $30$.

variants.statement: If $f(x) = 4 \cdot 3^x$, find $f(-2)$.
variants.explanation: The negative exponent means a reciprocal: $f(-2) = 4 \cdot \frac{1}{3^{2}} = \frac{4}{9}$. Multiplying instead of exponentiating gives $-24$.

variants.statement: If $f(x) = 5 \cdot 2^x$, find $f(3)$.

variants.explanation: The negative exponent means a reciprocal: $f(-2) = 3 \cdot \frac{1}{2^{2}} = \frac{3}{4}$. Multiplying instead of exponentiating gives $-12$.

variants.explanation: The negative exponent means a reciprocal: $f(-1) = 7 \cdot \frac{1}{3^{1}} = \frac{7}{3}$. Multiplying instead of exponentiating gives $-21$.

forms.title: Solving log equations
forms.skill: log_b(inner) = c means inner = b^c — rewrite in exponential form, then isolate x.

variants.statement: Solve for $x$: $\log_2(x - 5) = 3$.
variants.explanation: Rewrite in exponential form: $x - 5 = 2^{3} = 8$, so $x = 13$. Multiplying base times exponent — $2 \cdot 3 + 5$ — gives $11$.

variants.statement: Solve for $x$: $\log_3(x + 4) = 2$.
variants.explanation: Rewrite in exponential form: $x + 4 = 3^{2} = 9$, so $x = 5$. Multiplying base times exponent — $3 \cdot 2 - 4$ — gives $2$.

variants.statement: Solve for $x$: $\log_5(x - 1) = 2$.
variants.explanation: Rewrite in exponential form: $x - 1 = 5^{2} = 25$, so $x = 26$. Multiplying base times exponent — $5 \cdot 2 + 1$ — gives $11$.

variants.statement: Solve for $x$: $\log_2(x + 3) = 5$.
variants.explanation: Rewrite in exponential form: $x + 3 = 2^{5} = 32$, so $x = 29$. Multiplying base times exponent — $2 \cdot 5 - 3$ — gives $7$.

variants.statement: Solve for $x$: $\log_3(x - 2) = 2$.
variants.explanation: Rewrite in exponential form: $x - 2 = 3^{2} = 9$, so $x = 11$. Multiplying base times exponent — $3 \cdot 2 + 2$ — gives $8$.

variants.statement: Solve for $x$: $\log_5(x + 2) = 2$.
variants.explanation: Rewrite in exponential form: $x + 2 = 5^{2} = 25$, so $x = 23$. Multiplying base times exponent — $5 \cdot 2 - 2$ — gives $8$.

variants.statement: Solve for $x$: $\log_2(x - 7) = 5$.
variants.explanation: Rewrite in exponential form: $x - 7 = 2^{5} = 32$, so $x = 39$. Multiplying base times exponent — $2 \cdot 5 + 7$ — gives $17$.

variants.statement: Solve for $x$: $\log_3(x - 1) = 4$.
variants.explanation: Rewrite in exponential form: $x - 1 = 3^{4} = 81$, so $x = 82$. Multiplying base times exponent — $3 \cdot 4 + 1$ — gives $13$.

variants.statement: Solve for $x$: $\log_5(x - 4) = 3$.
variants.explanation: Rewrite in exponential form: $x - 4 = 5^{3} = 125$, so $x = 129$. Multiplying base times exponent — $5 \cdot 3 + 4$ — gives $19$.

variants.statement: Solve for $x$: $\log_2(x + 1) = 6$.
variants.explanation: Rewrite in exponential form: $x + 1 = 2^{6} = 64$, so $x = 63$. Multiplying base times exponent — $2 \cdot 6 - 1$ — gives $11$.

variants.statement: Solve for $x$: $\log_3(x + 5) = 2$.
variants.explanation: Rewrite in exponential form: $x + 5 = 3^{2} = 9$, so $x = 4$. Multiplying base times exponent — $3 \cdot 2 - 5$ — gives $1$.

variants.statement: Solve for $x$: $\log_5(x - 3) = 2$.
variants.explanation: Rewrite in exponential form: $x - 3 = 5^{2} = 25$, so $x = 28$. Multiplying base times exponent — $5 \cdot 2 + 3$ — gives $13$.

variants.statement: Evaluate exactly: $\sin\dfrac{\pi}{6}$.
variants.explanation: The reference angle and the quadrant's sign give $\sin\dfrac{\pi}{6} = \frac{1}{2}$. Quoting the co-function value $\cos\dfrac{\pi}{6} = \frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}$ is the classic swap.

forms.title: Terminal points on the unit circle
forms.skill: The terminal point of t is (cos t; sin t) — cosine first, signs from the quadrant.

variants.statement: The terminal point of $t = \dfrac{\pi}{6}$ on the unit circle is which of the following?
variants.explanation: The terminal point is $(\cos t; \sin t)$: cosine first, then sine, with signs set by the quadrant — here $(\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}; \frac{1}{2})$. Writing the pair as $(\sin t; \cos t)$ is the classic swap.

forms.title: General solutions of tan x = c
forms.skill: Tangent's period is 180°: one family x = θ + k·180° catches every solution.

variants.statement: Which of the following lists ALL solutions of $\tan x = \sqrt{3}$?
variants.explanation: Tangent repeats every $180°$, so one family covers every solution: x = 60° + k \cdot 180°. Using the period $360°$ — right for sine and cosine, wrong for tangent — misses half the answers.

forms.skill: On [0°, 360°): |c| < 1 gives two solutions, |c| = 1 gives one, |c| > 1 gives none.

variants.statement: How many solutions does $\sin x = 0$ have on the interval $[0°, 360°)$?
variants.explanation: On $[0°, 360°)$ the horizontal line cuts one full wave of $\sin$ twice: the count is $2$. Counting an endpoint twice (or forgetting a quadrant) shifts the answer by one.

variants.statement: How many solutions does $\sin x = 1$ have on the interval $[0°, 360°)$?
variants.explanation: On $[0°, 360°)$ the graph of $\sin$ touches this extreme value exactly once per period: the count is $1$. Counting an endpoint twice (or forgetting a quadrant) shifts the answer by one.

variants.statement: How many solutions does $\sin x = -1$ have on the interval $[0°, 360°)$?
variants.explanation: On $[0°, 360°)$ the graph of $\sin$ touches this extreme value exactly once per period: the count is $1$. Counting an endpoint twice (or forgetting a quadrant) shifts the answer by one.

variants.statement: How many solutions does $\sin x = 2$ have on the interval $[0°, 360°)$?
variants.explanation: On $[0°, 360°)$ the value lies outside $[-1, 1]$, beyond the range of $\sin$, so there are no solutions: the count is $0$. Counting an endpoint twice (or forgetting a quadrant) shifts the answer by one.

forms.title: Reading circle equations
forms.skill: (x − h)² + (y − k)² = r²: center (h; k) against the signs, radius √(right side).

variants.statement: The circle $(x - 2)^2 + (y + 3)^2 = 16$ has its center at which point?
variants.explanation: The standard form $(x - h)^2 + (y - k)^2 = r^2$ hides the center behind minus signs: here $(2; -3)$. Reading the written signs literally gives $(-2; 3)$ — the classic trap.

variants.statement: The circle $(x + 1)^2 + (y - 4)^2 = 49$ has its center at which point?
variants.explanation: The standard form $(x - h)^2 + (y - k)^2 = r^2$ hides the center behind minus signs: here $(-1; 4)$. Reading the written signs literally gives $(1; -4)$ — the classic trap.

variants.statement: The circle $(x - 5)^2 + (y - 2)^2 = 9$ has its center at which point?

variants.statement: The circle $(x + 4)^2 + (y + 6)^2 = 25$ has its center at which point?
variants.explanation: The standard form $(x - h)^2 + (y - k)^2 = r^2$ hides the center behind minus signs: here $(-4; -6)$. Reading the written signs literally gives $(4; 6)$ — the classic trap.

variants.statement: The circle $(x - 3)^2 + (y - 7)^2 = 36$ has its center at which point?
variants.explanation: The standard form $(x - h)^2 + (y - k)^2 = r^2$ hides the center behind minus signs: here $(3; 7)$. Reading the written signs literally gives $(-3; -7)$ — the classic trap.

variants.statement: The circle $(x + 2)^2 + (y - 1)^2 = 64$ has its center at which point?
variants.explanation: The standard form $(x - h)^2 + (y - k)^2 = r^2$ hides the center behind minus signs: here $(-2; 1)$. Reading the written signs literally gives $(2; -1)$ — the classic trap.

variants.statement: What is the radius of the circle $(x - 1)^2 + (y + 2)^2 = 49$?
variants.explanation: The right side is $r^2$, so $r = \sqrt{49} = 7$. Answering $49$ forgets to take the square root.

variants.explanation: The right side is $r^2$, so $r = \sqrt{25} = 5$. Answering $25$ forgets to take the square root.

variants.explanation: The right side is $r^2$, so $r = \sqrt{16} = 4$. Answering $16$ forgets to take the square root.

forms.title: Horizontal parabolas and foci
forms.skill: x = ay² opens sideways with the sign of a; y² = 4px puts the focus at (p; 0).

variants.statement: The parabola $x = 2y^2$ opens in which direction?
variants.explanation: With $x$ expressed in terms of $y^2$ the parabola is horizontal, and the positive coefficient $2$ points it right. "Upward/downward" belongs to $y = ax^2$ — the classic axis mix-up.

variants.statement: The parabola $x = -3y^2$ opens in which direction?
variants.explanation: With $x$ expressed in terms of $y^2$ the parabola is horizontal, and the negative coefficient $-3$ points it left. "Upward/downward" belongs to $y = ax^2$ — the classic axis mix-up.

variants.statement: The parabola $y^2 = 4x$ has its focus at which point?
variants.explanation: Match $y^2 = 4px$: $4p = 4$ gives $p = 1$, so the focus is $(1; 0)$ on the axis of symmetry. Forgetting to divide by 4 puts it at $(4; 0)$.

forms.title: Foci of an ellipse
forms.skill: c² = a² − b² for an ellipse — subtract the squares, then take the root.

variants.statement: For the ellipse $\dfrac{x^2}{25} + \dfrac{y^2}{16} = 1$, the foci are at $(\pm c; 0)$. Find $c$.
variants.explanation: For an ellipse $c^2 = a^2 - b^2 = 25 - 16 = 9$, so $c = 3$. Subtracting the radii themselves, $a - b = 1$, is the classic slip — the subtraction happens on the squares.
